feed.title: HealthRankings — Health News
feed.subtitle: Latest US health headlines. Full list: https://www.healthrankings.co/healthrankings-news.html

entry.title: Scientists Say These 8 Common Food Ingredients Are Linked to Heart Disease Risk
entry.summary: New research suggests that common food preservatives could raise your blood pressure. Here’s why.

entry.title: People are battling ‘ghost fat’ after losing weight on GLP-1s
entry.summary: Sometimes, seeing isn’t believing.

entry.title: The most hopeful cancer news in years
entry.summary: US cancer deaths are down 34% since 1991 — nearly 5 million lives saved. Inside the ASCO ovation and the wider turn in cancer medicine

entry.title: A veteran didn't think much of her forgetfulness, until her arm started to shake: "A life-changing disease"
entry.summary: Dr. Sara Whittingham thought she would know if something was wrong. But her minor symptoms had a surprising cause.

entry.title: I Ate at Least 20 Grams of Protein at Breakfast for a Month—Here Are the Benefits I Noticed
entry.summary: A food writer tries eating a high-protein breakfast for an entire month, and shares how this change improved her energy, hunger and snacking habits.

entry.title: Podiatrists Share What They'd Never Do (And Wish You Wouldn't Either)
entry.summary: Experts weigh in on the behaviors to avoid to keep your feet and ankles healthy and functioning.

entry.title: Doctors Are Begging Middle-Aged Women To Make This 1 Lifestyle Change ASAP
entry.summary: If you're between ages 40 to 65, this singular habit can boost bone health, mood, longevity and more — yet not enough people prioritize it.

entry.title: 5 Foods That Aren't Doing Your Cholesterol Any Favors
entry.summary: And why certain lifestyle choices may have even more impact than the food you eat.

entry.title: This Thumb Test May Indicate If You're At Risk For A Heart Problem
entry.summary: While it's not a diagnostic tool, experts say it might provide clues about your risk for an aortic aneurysm.

entry.title: Can Jamun help control blood sugar? Expert explains its benefits for people with diabetes
entry.summary: Every Indian summer brings with it a familiar sight. Street vendors stack baskets filled with shiny purple jamuns, while families discuss age-old remedies involving the fruit and its seeds. For many households, jamun is more than just a seasonal treat. It is …

entry.title: After decades of pelvic pain and 100-plus doctor visits, one question changed it all
entry.summary: Jabbing pangs and constant headaches followed him for years, but the blood tests always came back normal — until one day a specialist thought to ask something new.

entry.title: Young colon cancer linked to specific fats in ultra-processed foods
entry.summary: Young colon cancer may not be not the same disease doctors are used to treating. Researchers are discovering new links to modern diets and gut toxins.

entry.title: I led the U.S. CDC response to the 2014 Ebola epidemic. The new outbreak needs massive, immediate, meticulous action
entry.summary: “Ebola is an unforgiving enemy,” writes Tom Frieden, who led the U.S. CDC response to the 2014-2016 Ebola outbreak.

entry.title: Hearing is widely believed to be the last sense to fade, and a 2020 University of British Columbia EEG study on dying hospice patients found their brains still responded to sound in the final hours of unresponsiveness, suggesting the voices of loved ones in the
entry.summary: A 2020 University of British Columbia EEG study on dying hospice patients found their brains still responded to sound in the final hours of unresponsiveness, giving neural weight to the hospice tradition of telling families to keep talking.

entry.title: Congo outbreak could rival the largest Ebola epidemic on record, CDC warns
entry.summary: In one worst-case scenario projected, more than 20,000 cases would occur within three months if only 1 in 5 infected people are identified and isolated within two days.

entry.title: Tick season arrives early in Central New York as experts urge precautions
entry.summary: Tick season is off to an early and active start across Central New York and experts say rising outdoor activity is only increasing the risk of exposure.

entry.title: Learning to Cook Could Lower Your Dementia Risk By 70%, New Study Says
entry.summary: Home cooking just once a week may help lower dementia risk, new research shows. Learn the brain health benefits and simple ways to get started.

entry.title: Parkinson’s Disease Is Consistently Linked to Pesticide Exposure. Farmworkers — and Nearby Communities — Are at Risk in Texas’ Rio Grande Valley.
entry.summary: By Daisy Yuhas This story is part of a series by Public Health Watch and MyRGV.com. Puente News Collaborative is a partner in this collaboration. MCALLEN, Texas — When neuroscientist Kelsey Baker hears the low buzz of planes over her home in the Rio Grande Va…

entry.title: Senior NIH official pushes MAHA strategy to skeptical ADA audience
entry.summary: An NIH advisor told researchers gathered at the American Diabetes Association conference that MAHA goals match the agency's priorities.

entry.title: Fasting after 60 changes more than waistlines, exposing a trade-off many dieters never see coming
entry.summary: Most folks know intermittent fasting helps with weight loss, usually by limiting your daily eating window or cutting calories a couple of times a week. But does your age change how well this works for you—and might there be some hidden dangers?

entry.title: New York’s Latest Tourist? A Rare, Potentially More Severe Form of Lyme Disease
entry.summary: This week, health officials reported the first known case of Borrelia mayonii in New York.

entry.title: Modeled Scenario Projections for the Ebola Disease Outbreak Caused by Bundibugyo Virus, 2026
entry.summary: This report describes using a transmission model to project Ebola outbreak growth over three months.

entry.title: New Colorectal Cancer Breakthrough Could Eradicate Tumors Without Surgery
entry.summary: Immunotherapy wipes out some colorectal tumors without surgery, signaling a major shift toward less invasive treatment.

entry.title: Early Life Diet Linked to Adolescent Intelligence
entry.summary: A new study links poor infant diet to lower adolescent intelligence, outlining new research principles.

entry.title: New solar-power desalination device leaves no brine
entry.summary: University of Rochester researchers have built a solar-powered desalination device that doesn't leave behind brine — the concentrated saltwater that conventional plants dump back into the ocean, where it raises…

entry.title: Why autism symptoms can ease during a fever — and how to mimic it
entry.summary: Parents and caregivers have long reported that when some autistic people run a fever from an infection, their autism-related symptoms ease for a while. MIT and Harvard Medical School researchers,…

entry.title: Some people lose over 20% of their body weight on Ozempic. Others lose almost nothing. A doctor explains why
entry.summary: The same drug can strip over 20% off one body and barely touch another. A doctor explains the genetics, biology, and habits that decide who responds to...

entry.title: 100 Years Ago, Thousands Fell Into a Deep Sleep With No Explanation. Today, It’s Still a Mystery.
entry.summary: Experts can’t agree on the cause of an Encephalitis lethargica outbreak.

entry.title: Researchers Are Using AI to Create Vaccines—and It’s Working
entry.summary: pEVAC-PS is the first experimental vaccine designed completely with AI to be tested in a human trial.

entry.title: Ebola figures appear to offer hope as case numbers drop in DR Congo but it's not that simple
entry.summary: There are now 380 confirmed cases of Ebola in DR Congo, far lower than initial estimates of suspected cases, writes Fergus Walsh.

entry.title: South Africa rolls out game-changing HIV shot amid funding shortfalls
entry.summary: A new twice-yearly HIV prevention injection could transform South Africa's fight against the epidemic — but U.S. aid cuts and limited doses threaten to slow its impact.

entry.title: This Vitamin May Lower Your Stroke Risk by 17%, According to Research
entry.summary: Learn what a narrative review found regarding vitamin D and heart disease. Plus, how to ensure you get enough of the sunshine vitamin.

entry.title: Innovation in medicine is having a breakthrough moment
entry.summary: Recent weeks have featured a series of positive developments.

entry.title: A healthier gut may be key to cancer care
entry.summary: Dubbed “the next frontier of cancer prevention and care,” the microbiome appears to be especially critical when it comes to immunotherapy.

entry.title: Exclusive: Biggest Pancreatic Cancer Breakthrough In 50 Years, Says Indian Doctor, Co-Author Of Global Clinical Trial
entry.summary: Pancreatic cancer is one of the most severe, aggressive, and deadly forms of cancer with a high mortality rate. Research over the years indicates that pancreat

entry.title: The World Cup vs. bugs, germs and heat: Here's the game plan
entry.summary: Despite a diminished federal presence, public health departments are preparing for common ailments that could afflict fans who gather for the event — and are keeping an eye on the Ebola outbreak, too.

entry.title: Heart transplant gives Hays teen a new lease on life; GoFundMe
entry.summary: Kaylee Stuart of Hays with her sister on May 21, days after her heart transplant at C

entry.title: My Husband Has Just Two Years to Live. He Wants Us to Lie to His Children About the Details
entry.summary: I don't like lying.

entry.title: Blueprint of a cure: The rare people who are invulnerable to HIV
entry.summary: Scientists are studying the few extraordinary individuals whose bodies seem able to naturally defend themselves from HIV in the hope of finding new cures.

entry.title: Top Doctor Demands Answers Over Trump Health Concerns
entry.summary: The White House is still being coy about the president’s big checkup.

entry.title: An avocado a day may help control blood sugar, study claims
entry.summary: Eating one avocado per day may help lower dietary glycemic load, according to the findings of a recent study.

entry.title: No red meat, no dairy, and no end in sight: How a tick-borne allergy has transformed Martha’s Vineyard
entry.summary: Lone star ticks that carry alpha gal syndrome have taken over the summer playground of presidents and celebrities.

entry.title: The biggest collagen study yet reveals what actually works
entry.summary: A major review of nearly 8,000 participants found that collagen supplements can improve skin health and ease osteoarthritis symptoms, especially when taken consistently over longer periods. Researchers also found modest benefits for muscle and tendon health. …

entry.title: Ebola Pioneer Peter Piot on How Worried the World Should Be
entry.summary: The scientist who helped discover Ebola in 1976 says the latest outbreak poses a serious threat in Central Africa — but fears of a pandemic are misplaced.

entry.title: Popular Weight Loss Drugs Linked to Lower Risk of Breast Cancer
entry.summary: The potential benefits of GLP-1 drugs, like Ozempic or Wegovy, are stacking up.

entry.title: A new type of lyme disease found in Upstate New York according to CDC
entry.summary: A report published by the CDC on Thursday said acase of B. mayonii Lyme disease was detected in upstate New York last July.

entry.title: State health officials investigate possible cancer cluster at Uxbridge High School
entry.summary: Massachusetts health officials are investigating a possible cancer cluster at Uxbridge High School after several teachers were diagnosed with breast cancer.

entry.title: U of A study links 3 sleep habits to brain aging signs
entry.summary: TUCSON, Ariz. (KVOA) - A University of Arizona study has linked three common sleep habits to markers of brain aging that could increase dementia risk.

entry.title: Milwaukee Health Department monitoring 3 for Ebola
entry.summary: MILWAUKEE (WKOW) -- The City of Milwaukee Health Department is monitoring three individuals considered to be at very low risk for Ebola as of June 4.

entry.title: Summer sun fails to fix vitamin D gap in at risk groups
entry.summary: Vitamin D levels remain low all year-round in key at-risk groups in England, challenging the belief that summer sunlight is enough to restore them.
